page: Amazon Web Services m5.4xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with an Intel Xeon 8175M processor running at 3.1 GHz. The configuration includes 16 dedicated vCPUs (8 physical cores with 2 threads per core) and 64.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, yielding 4.0 GB of RAM per core. Cache resources consist of 32 KB L1d, 1.0 MB L2, and 33 MB L3 cache. The instance lacks local storage and dedicated GPU hardware, relying entirely on network-attached storage and offering a baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps.

Benchmark data reveals a clear performance split. Single-core processing is weak, falling into the bottom 25% for stress-ng single-core, Geekbench single-core, and single-threaded compression tests. However, multi-core performance scales to average levels, as demonstrated by middle-50% rankings in stress-ng multi-core, Geekbench multi-core, and Passmark CPU tests. Memory bandwidth for both cached and uncached operations remains average, with uncached read speeds at 87.77 GB/sec and write speeds at 45.25 GB/sec. For specialized tasks, the CPU supports AVX and AVX-512F instructions, though it lacks newer extensions like AMX or AVX-512 VNNI.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m5.4xlarge offers a balanced resource density of 4 GB of RAM per vCPU, making it a standard option for general-purpose workloads that do not require high single-thread velocity. It delivers average throughput for database operations, static web serving, and Redis SET operations. This profile makes the instance appropriate for multi-threaded applications, web serving, containerized microservices, and moderate database workloads, whereas single-threaded tasks or large-scale LLM inference will encounter performance bottlenecks.
description: Amazon Web Services m5.4xlarge is an x86_64 general-purpose instance running on the Nitro hypervisor. It features an Intel Xeon 8175M processor with 16 dedicated vCPUs, 8 physical cores, and 64.0 GB of DDR4 memory. The hardware configuration lacks local storage and GPU accelerators, relying on a 5 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. Performance benchmarks indicate a notable tradeoff: single-core CPU execution and single-threaded compression tasks rank in the bottom 25%, while multi-core performance, memory bandwidth, and database operations achieve average, middle-50% marks. The instance supports AVX and AVX-512F instructions but lacks newer AMX capabilities. Qualitatively, the 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio provides balanced resource density for multi-threaded workloads. It is best suited for general-purpose applications, static web serving, Redis deployments, and moderate database operations that leverage multi-core scaling rather than raw single-threaded speed.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m5.4xlarge is a general-purpose instance with 16 vCPUs, 64 GB RAM, and an Intel Xeon CPU. It offers average multi-core performance but exhibits weak single-core benchmark results.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m5.4xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 16 vCPUs, 64 GB DDR4 RAM, and average multi-core performance.
tagline: A balanced general-purpose virtual machine offering stable multi-core scaling and standard memory density for non-intensive enterprise workloads.
bullet_points: Intel Xeon 8175M processor with 16 dedicated vCPUs and 64 GB DDR4 RAM
Average multi-core performance contrasted with weak single-core execution
No local storage and 5 Gbps baseline network bandwidth
Supports AVX and AVX-512F instruction set extensions
Suitable for web serving, Redis, and multi-threaded database workloads
